We start with a thin horizontal. line below the middle of the screen. That's what this clip path creates. Then. we animate to a full screen rectangle. The animation runs for 2 seconds. I'm. using a custom cubic basier curve. Same. as the custom is property we created. The key part is the pseudo element. property set to view transitions new. route. This tells the browser to apply. this animation to the new page as it. transitions in. Next, I'll create a. handle navigation function that manages. our link clicks. It takes a path. parameter and returns an event handler. function. First, it checks if we are. clicking a link to the current page. If. so, it prevents the default action and. returns early no need to transition to. the same page. Otherwise, it uses a. transition router to navigate to the new. path. The important part is the on. transition ready call back. This. triggers our page transition animation. when the new page is ready to show. Finally, let's update our JSX to use.

this navigation handler. I'll add an. onclick event listener to each link. component in our navigation. With these. changes, clicking any navigation link. will now trigger our custom page. transition. effect. But there is one issue. When the. route changes, you can see the current. page still disappears immediately, which. isn't what we want. To fix this, we need. to make some updates to our CSS to. control the default behavior of the view. transitions. Let's add the CSS rules. Now, first, I'm targeting both the old. and new root elements. I'm setting their. animation property to none and override. the default animations. This gives us. complete control over how the transition. behaves. Next, for the view transition. group, I'm setting Z-index to auto. Again, overriding it. This ensures our. transition elements stack correctly with. other elements on the page. For the view. transition image pair, I'm setting. isolation to isolate. This creates a new. stacking context so our animations don't. interfere with each other. I'm alsoing. will change clip path to optimize the. performance for our clip path. animations. And setting zindex to one to. position it above normal content. For. the view transition new route, I'm. setting a high zindex of 10,000. This. ensures the new page content always. appears on top during the transition. Again, I'm setting animation to none to. disable default animations. Finally, for. view transition old route, I'm setting. Zindex to one. This positions the old. content below the new page during the. transitions and again disabling the. default animations. If you want a deeper. explanation of these CSS rules, I. covered them a bit more in detail in my. previous Nex.js page transition video. I'll drop the link in the description. With these CSS fixes in place, our page. transition is now ready. Now, just to. keep this video focused and concise, I. am not including the text animation. aspects. Those animations have nothing. to do with the page transition itself. However, if you're interested in. learning how to create those text. animations, check out my recent videos. on GAP split text animations, you can.

simply copy and paste that code with. different delay values to recreate the. effect. And that wraps up our tutorial. I hope you found the video helpful. See. you in the next one.

====

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OZNeMvREBQU

Discover how to leverage Next.js App Router, GSAP, and the View Transitions API to create buttery-smooth page transitions that elevate your web projects from basic to breathtaking!

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Zajno

Website: https://zajno.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.description:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講述者提到，如果觀眾有持續關注這個頻道，應該已經對影片中的網站相當熟悉了。 幾個月前，頻道曾探討過該網站的著陸頁面揭示動畫，以及引人注目的文字覆蓋效果。 然而，還有許多令人印象深刻的元素尚未涵蓋，例如令人驚嘆的頁面過渡動畫。 講述者也提到，許多觀眾使用 Next.js 建立網站，並且對之前的頁面過渡影片反響熱烈。 這次的影片將分享如何使用 CSS 屬性和 GSAP 建立流暢的頁面過渡效果，並提供原始碼連結。

頁面結構


接著，影片示範如何在 Next.js 專案中建立頁面結構。 首先，清理了預設程式碼，包括刪除全域 CSS 檔案和頁面模組 CSS 檔案中的預設樣式。 然後，在主頁面組件中創建了一個包含標題和英雄圖片的 div。 接著，建立了工作頁面、工作室頁面和聯絡頁面，每個頁面都包含不同的內容和佈局，例如標題和圖片。

導航欄


接下來，影片介紹了如何建立導航欄。 首先，在來源目錄中建立一個 components 資料夾，並在其中新增一個 nav.tsx 檔案。 這個檔案包含一個簡單的導航組件，其中包含 logo、導航項目和一些虛擬文本。 接著，將導航欄加入到 layout.js 檔案中，使其在所有頁面上渲染。

CSS 樣式


影片接著講解了 CSS 樣式設定。 首先，建立顏色變數，設定淺色主題，包括白色背景和深色文字。 接著，使用重置樣式，去除預設邊距和填充，並使用 border box 尺寸設定。 之後，設定字體樣式，包括標題和文本的不同字體大小。 此外，也設定了導航欄的樣式，使其固定在視窗頂部，並使用 flexbox 分割成兩欄。 影片中也示範了如何設定各個頁面的特定佈局，例如首頁的全螢幕標題和英雄圖片，工作頁面的居中佈局和項目，以及工作室和聯絡頁面的兩欄佈局。 最後，也提到了一些用於文字動畫的工具類別，但強調重點是頁面過渡效果。

揭示元件


影片接下來介紹了創建過渡效果的關鍵元素，也就是揭示元件（revealer）。 講述者描述了如何添加 CSS 來設定這個元件，包含定位、尺寸、變換原點和顏色。 揭示元件最初在螢幕上完全可見，然後使用 GSAP 進行動畫處理，以創造平滑的過渡效果。

自訂 Hook


為了避免在每個頁面重複動畫程式碼，影片提到了使用自訂 hook 來封裝動畫邏輯。 這個 hook 被命名為 `useRevealer`，它使用了 GSAP 和一些自訂參數。 講述者解釋了如何設定這個 hook，包括導入必要的模組、註冊自訂插件，以及創建自訂 easing 曲線。 接著，在 hook 內使用 `useGSAP` hook，並設定动画，使其将揭示元件的 Y 軸缩放属性从 1 缩放到 0，以產生動畫效果。
================================================

The. first column will simply have an H2 with. some text. The second column will also. have placeholder text using an H2. element to make this page look a bit. different. I'll also add an image inside. the second column. That's it for the. studio page. Next, let's create a new. folder called contact and add a page JSX. file. The contact page will also have a. similar layout to the studio page. We'll. split it into two columns. The first. column will contain an H2 heading. In. the second column, we'll add some. placeholder text inside a div with the. class contact. copy. I'll also add some dummy text. style to look like social. links. That completes our contact. page. Now you can see all our pages are. ready and can be accessed through their. appropriate routes. Let's move on to. setting up the. navigation. I'll create a new folder.

called components inside the source. directory. Inside this folder, I'll add. a new file called. nav.tjsx. First, I'll import the link. component from nex.js. This will also be. a simple functional component called. nav. For the structure, I'll create a. main div with the class nav. Inside. this, I'll add two columns using divs. with the class column. The first column. will contain our logo. I'll create a div. with the class nav logo and add a link. that points to the homepage. In the. second column, I'll add the navigation. items and some dummy text. The nav items. will hold all our page links. Each. navigation item gets its own div with. the class nav item. Inside each nav. item, I'll add a link component that. points to the appropriate page. Our. first link will go to the work page. The. second link will go to the studio page. and the third will go to the contact. page. Below the navigation items,. highlight some dummy copy with the class. of nav copy just to match the look of. the original website. There we go. Our. navigation is ready to be used across. all pages. Now, let's actually add the. navigation to our pages. I'll go to the. layout.js file and import our nav. component at the top. I'll add it inside. the body tag so it gets rendered on all. pages. There we have it. We now have all. our pages with the nav bar fully. functional. Let's get to styling. now. Let's quickly handle the styling. Now I add some basic CSS to give our. site a clean look. I'm not going to. dwell on this part too much since it's. not related to our page transitions. Starting with the color variables. I'm. setting up a simple light theme with a. white background and dark text. Next. comes our reset styles. Zeroing out. margins and padding and using border box. sizing for more predictable layouts. For. typography, I'm using a custom font. throughout the site with different sizes. for headings and text. Our H1 gets a. large font size with some negative. letter spacing for that modern look. The. H2s are smaller but still maintain that. same design language. Text elements like. links and paragraphs share same styling,. no underlines, dark color and slightly.

bold weight. For the navigation, I am. fixing it to the top of the viewport. with full width. I have split the nav. into two columns using flexbox. The. first takes up one/ird of the space and. the second takes 2/3. Each page gets its. own specific layout. The homepage fills. the viewport with the main heading. positioned near the top center. I'm making the hero image quite large. and centering it at the bottom of the. [Music]. screen. The work page gets a centered. layout with projects stacked in a column. at 32% width. For studio and contact pages, I'm using. a two column layout where the left. column is narrower than the. right. Both maintain consistent padding. and spacing between elements. Finally, I have added some utility. classes for the text animations. These. will work with Gap's split text plug-in,. but you can skip this part as the video. will be only covering the page. transitions. With our styling done, the. site now has a clean, minimal aesthetic. that will showcase our transitions. nicely. Now, let's create the key. element for our transition effect. If. you watched the demo closely, you. noticed how each transition begins with. a dark overlay that reveals the new page. content. To achieve this effect, I have. come up with a simple solution, a div. element that we will animate using scale. transforms. We'll call this element our. revealer. Let me add the CSS for this. element. Now, I'm positioning it fixed. on the screen. Starting from the top. left corner, it needs to cover the. entire viewport. So, I'm setting both. width and height to full screen. dimensions. The transform origin is set. to center top. This is crucial because. it determines how our scaling animation. will behave. For the color, I'm using. our foreground variable which is nearly. black. I have set pointer events to. none. And finally, a zindex of two. ensures it sits above our content. Now,. we need to add this regular element to. each of our pages. When a new page. mounts, this overlay will initially be. visible creating that covered state. Then, we'll animate it using GAP to.

create our smooth transition effect. This simple div is actually the secret. behind the entire transition. Just shows. how powerful CSS transforms can be when. combined with good animation. timing. Now, we need to animate our. reveler element when a new page loads. We could create a gap animation directly. on each page, but that would mean. repeating the same code multiple times. Instead, let's create a custom hook that. encapsulates this animation logic. We'll. call it use reer. This approach will. make our code much cleaner and easier to. maintain. If you want to change the. animation later, we only need to update. it in one place. Let's start by adding. the use client directive at the top of. our file. This directive is necessary. because we are using browser APIs. through gap. It tells nextJS that this. code should only run on the client side,. not during the server side rendering. So. you would need to define this directive,. otherwise it will throw errors. Next,. I'll import the tools we need from GSAP. the use gap hook for react integration,. the main gap object and customize for. more control over our animation timing. Now I need to register the custom is. plug-in with gap so we can use it. I'm. also creating a custom using cow called. hop with a cubic pzier cow. This creates. that smooth elegant motion that gives. our transition that polished feel. Now. for our actual oak function use revealer. inside I'm using the use gap hook which. automatically sets up and cleans up gap. animations. The animation itself is. simple. We are targeting the revealer. glass and animating its scale Y property. from one down to zero. This creates the. effect of the dark overlay retracting. upward. I have set the duration to 1.25. seconds for a smooth feel and added a. delay of 1 second to accommodate the. clip path and scale animations we'll. cover next. Finally, I'm using our. custom hop easing for the distinctive. motion. Now we just need to go to each. of our page components, import this. hook, and call. it. When each page loads, the revealer. will automatically animate out toward. the top, revealing our page content,. initiating the transition.

effect. Now let's create the key part of. the transition. The part where the new. page content reveals itself. First we. need to install a library called next. review transitions. This library makes. it easier to implement view transitions. API with next.js app router. Once it's. installed let's update our nav. component. We'll start by adding the use. client directive at the top since we are. using browser APIs. Next I'll import two. key hooks. Use transition router from. next view transitions and use path name. from next.js. The use transition router. hook will give us control over page. transitions and use path name will tell. us which page we are currently on. This. is important because we don't want to. trigger transitions when clicking on the. current pages link. Inside our nav. component, I'll initialize this hooks. I'm creating a router object with use. transition router and getting the. current path name with use path name. hook. Now let's create a function called. trigger page transition. This is where. the magic happens. I'm using the web. animations API to animate the documents. root element. The animation uses clip. path to create that expanding rectangle. effect. We start with a thin horizontal. line below the middle of the screen. That's what this clip path creates. Then. we animate to a full screen rectangle. The animation runs for 2 seconds. I'm. using a custom cubic basier curve. Same. as the custom is property we created. The key part is the pseudo element. property set to view transitions new. route. This tells the browser to apply. this animation to the new page as it. transitions in. Next, I'll create a. handle navigation function that manages. our link clicks. It takes a path. parameter and returns an event handler. function. First, it checks if we are. clicking a link to the current page. If. so, it prevents the default action and. returns early no need to transition to. the same page. Otherwise, it uses a. transition router to navigate to the new. path. The important part is the on. transition ready call back. This. triggers our page transition animation. when the new page is ready to show. Finally, let's update our JSX to use.

this navigation handler. I'll add an. onclick event listener to each link. component in our navigation. With these. changes, clicking any navigation link. will now trigger our custom page. transition. effect. But there is one issue. When the. route changes, you can see the current. page still disappears immediately, which. isn't what we want. To fix this, we need. to make some updates to our CSS to. control the default behavior of the view. transitions. Let's add the CSS rules. Now, first, I'm targeting both the old. and new root elements. I'm setting their. animation property to none and override. the default animations. This gives us. complete control over how the transition. behaves. Next, for the view transition. group, I'm setting Z-index to auto. Again, overriding it. This ensures our. transition elements stack correctly with. other elements on the page. For the view. transition image pair, I'm setting. isolation to isolate. This creates a new. stacking context so our animations don't. interfere with each other. I'm alsoing. will change clip path to optimize the. performance for our clip path. animations. And setting zindex to one to. position it above normal content. For. the view transition new route, I'm. setting a high zindex of 10,000. This. ensures the new page content always. appears on top during the transition. Again, I'm setting animation to none to. disable default animations. Finally, for. view transition old route, I'm setting. Zindex to one. This positions the old. content below the new page during the. transitions and again disabling the. default animations. If you want a deeper. explanation of these CSS rules, I. covered them a bit more in detail in my. previous Nex.js page transition video. I'll drop the link in the description. With these CSS fixes in place, our page. transition is now ready. Now, just to. keep this video focused and concise, I. am not including the text animation. aspects. Those animations have nothing. to do with the page transition itself. However, if you're interested in. learning how to create those text. animations, check out my recent videos. on GAP split text animations, you can.

simply copy and paste that code with. different delay values to recreate the. effect. And that wraps up our tutorial. I hope you found the video helpful. See. you in the next one.

====

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OZNeMvREBQU

Discover how to leverage Next.js App Router, GSAP, and the View Transitions API to create buttery-smooth page transitions that elevate your web projects from basic to breathtaking!

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Zajno

Website: https://zajno.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.content:encoded:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講述者提到，如果觀眾有持續關注這個頻道，應該已經對影片中的網站相當熟悉了。 幾個月前，頻道曾探討過該網站的著陸頁面揭示動畫，以及引人注目的文字覆蓋效果。 然而，還有許多令人印象深刻的元素尚未涵蓋，例如令人驚嘆的頁面過渡動畫。 講述者也提到，許多觀眾使用 Next.js 建立網站，並且對之前的頁面過渡影片反響熱烈。 這次的影片將分享如何使用 CSS 屬性和 GSAP 建立流暢的頁面過渡效果，並提供原始碼連結。

頁面結構


接著，影片示範如何在 Next.js 專案中建立頁面結構。 首先，清理了預設程式碼，包括刪除全域 CSS 檔案和頁面模組 CSS 檔案中的預設樣式。 然後，在主頁面組件中創建了一個包含標題和英雄圖片的 div。 接著，建立了工作頁面、工作室頁面和聯絡頁面，每個頁面都包含不同的內容和佈局，例如標題和圖片。

導航欄


接下來，影片介紹了如何建立導航欄。 首先，在來源目錄中建立一個 components 資料夾，並在其中新增一個 nav.tsx 檔案。 這個檔案包含一個簡單的導航組件，其中包含 logo、導航項目和一些虛擬文本。 接著，將導航欄加入到 layout.js 檔案中，使其在所有頁面上渲染。

CSS 樣式


影片接著講解了 CSS 樣式設定。 首先，建立顏色變數，設定淺色主題，包括白色背景和深色文字。 接著，使用重置樣式，去除預設邊距和填充，並使用 border box 尺寸設定。 之後，設定字體樣式，包括標題和文本的不同字體大小。 此外，也設定了導航欄的樣式，使其固定在視窗頂部，並使用 flexbox 分割成兩欄。 影片中也示範了如何設定各個頁面的特定佈局，例如首頁的全螢幕標題和英雄圖片，工作頁面的居中佈局和項目，以及工作室和聯絡頁面的兩欄佈局。 最後，也提到了一些用於文字動畫的工具類別，但強調重點是頁面過渡效果。

揭示元件


影片接下來介紹了創建過渡效果的關鍵元素，也就是揭示元件（revealer）。 講述者描述了如何添加 CSS 來設定這個元件，包含定位、尺寸、變換原點和顏色。 揭示元件最初在螢幕上完全可見，然後使用 GSAP 進行動畫處理，以創造平滑的過渡效果。

自訂 Hook


為了避免在每個頁面重複動畫程式碼，影片提到了使用自訂 hook 來封裝動畫邏輯。 這個 hook 被命名為 `useRevealer`，它使用了 GSAP 和一些自訂參數。 講述者解釋了如何設定這個 hook，包括導入必要的模組、註冊自訂插件，以及創建自訂 easing 曲線。 接著，在 hook 內使用 `useGSAP` hook，並設定动画，使其将揭示元件的 Y 軸缩放属性从 1 缩放到 0，以產生動畫效果。
================================================

If you have been following this channel,. you are probably familiar with this. website already. A few months back, we. explored the landing page reveal. animation and this eye-catching text. over effect from the same site. But. there is still plenty of impressive. elements we haven't covered yet. Take a. look at the stunning page transition. animation. The way the current page. holds its position, while the new page. glides into view really looks cool,. creating an eye-catching layered effect. Based on the response to my previous. page transition videos, I can tell many. of you are using Nex.js to build your. websites, and these are some of your. favorite content pieces. This particular. effect has been on my to-do list for a. while now, so the timing felt perfect to. break it down for you today. After. experimenting for several hours to. reverse engineer this animation, I came. up with my own implementation using a. combination of clip pads and CSS. transforms. While my version might not. be quite as polished as the original,. the process was really a great learning. experience, realizing what's possible. when you leverage GCSS properties. effectively. In this video, I'll walk. you through creating these smooth page. transitions in Nex.js with few. transitions and GSAP. If you find my. work helpful, don't forget to hit the. like button and consider subscribing if. you haven't already. Also, if you want. to access the source code for this. project, plus hundreds of other similar. micro projects along with a complete. website template every month, you can. check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's dive in. I have got a fresh nextjs. project running locally to save us time. Let's start by cleaning up the default. code to get a clean workspace. I'll open. global CSS file first and delete all the. default styles. I'll do the same with. page module CSS file, just removing. everything. Now for the homepage. component. I'll clear out all the. starter code from the main file and. start building our homepage. structure. I'll create a div with the. class home and add a header inside. The.

header will have an H1 with some sample. text. Next, I'll add a hero image using. a native HTML image. tag. Since we are working with images,. let's also add the images for this. project. I'll go to the public folder. and drop in the image files we need. There we go. Our homepage is all set. up. It's time to add more pages. Now,. inside the app directory, I'll create a. new folder called work and add a file. named. page.jsx. This will be a simple. functional component just like the. homepage. I'll create a div with the. class name work and add an h1 inside. To. create a project list layout, I'll add a. container called projects and place four. images inside it. The actual page. content doesn't matter much since it's. independent from the transition effect. I just want to make sure the page. doesn't look empty. That's our work page. done. Let's create another page called. studio. I'll follow the same steps. Inside the app directory, I'll create a. new folder called studio and add a file. named. page.jsx. This will be also a simple. functional component. Just like the. other pages, I'll create a div with the. class name studio. For this page, I'll. divide the layout into two columns. The. first column will simply have an H2 with. some text. The second column will also. have placeholder text using an H2. element to make this page look a bit. different. I'll also add an image inside. the second column. That's it for the. studio page. Next, let's create a new. folder called contact and add a page JSX. file. The contact page will also have a. similar layout to the studio page. We'll. split it into two columns. The first. column will contain an H2 heading. In. the second column, we'll add some. placeholder text inside a div with the. class contact. copy. I'll also add some dummy text. style to look like social. links. That completes our contact. page. Now you can see all our pages are. ready and can be accessed through their. appropriate routes. Let's move on to. setting up the. navigation. I'll create a new folder.

called components inside the source. directory. Inside this folder, I'll add. a new file called. nav.tjsx. First, I'll import the link. component from nex.js. This will also be. a simple functional component called. nav. For the structure, I'll create a. main div with the class nav. Inside. this, I'll add two columns using divs. with the class column. The first column. will contain our logo. I'll create a div. with the class nav logo and add a link. that points to the homepage. In the. second column, I'll add the navigation. items and some dummy text. The nav items. will hold all our page links. Each. navigation item gets its own div with. the class nav item. Inside each nav. item, I'll add a link component that. points to the appropriate page. Our. first link will go to the work page. The. second link will go to the studio page. and the third will go to the contact. page. Below the navigation items,. highlight some dummy copy with the class. of nav copy just to match the look of. the original website. There we go. Our. navigation is ready to be used across. all pages. Now, let's actually add the. navigation to our pages. I'll go to the. layout.js file and import our nav. component at the top. I'll add it inside. the body tag so it gets rendered on all. pages. There we have it. We now have all. our pages with the nav bar fully. functional. Let's get to styling. now. Let's quickly handle the styling. Now I add some basic CSS to give our. site a clean look. I'm not going to. dwell on this part too much since it's. not related to our page transitions. Starting with the color variables. I'm. setting up a simple light theme with a. white background and dark text. Next. comes our reset styles. Zeroing out. margins and padding and using border box. sizing for more predictable layouts. For. typography, I'm using a custom font. throughout the site with different sizes. for headings and text. Our H1 gets a. large font size with some negative. letter spacing for that modern look. The. H2s are smaller but still maintain that. same design language. Text elements like. links and paragraphs share same styling,. no underlines, dark color and slightly.

bold weight. For the navigation, I am. fixing it to the top of the viewport. with full width. I have split the nav. into two columns using flexbox. The. first takes up one/ird of the space and. the second takes 2/3. Each page gets its. own specific layout. The homepage fills. the viewport with the main heading. positioned near the top center. I'm making the hero image quite large. and centering it at the bottom of the. [Music]. screen. The work page gets a centered. layout with projects stacked in a column. at 32% width. For studio and contact pages, I'm using. a two column layout where the left. column is narrower than the. right. Both maintain consistent padding. and spacing between elements. Finally, I have added some utility. classes for the text animations. These. will work with Gap's split text plug-in,. but you can skip this part as the video. will be only covering the page. transitions. With our styling done, the. site now has a clean, minimal aesthetic. that will showcase our transitions. nicely. Now, let's create the key. element for our transition effect. If. you watched the demo closely, you. noticed how each transition begins with. a dark overlay that reveals the new page. content. To achieve this effect, I have. come up with a simple solution, a div. element that we will animate using scale. transforms. We'll call this element our. revealer. Let me add the CSS for this. element. Now, I'm positioning it fixed. on the screen. Starting from the top. left corner, it needs to cover the. entire viewport. So, I'm setting both. width and height to full screen. dimensions. The transform origin is set. to center top. This is crucial because. it determines how our scaling animation. will behave. For the color, I'm using. our foreground variable which is nearly. black. I have set pointer events to. none. And finally, a zindex of two. ensures it sits above our content. Now,. we need to add this regular element to. each of our pages. When a new page. mounts, this overlay will initially be. visible creating that covered state. Then, we'll animate it using GAP to.

create our smooth transition effect. This simple div is actually the secret. behind the entire transition. Just shows. how powerful CSS transforms can be when. combined with good animation. timing. Now, we need to animate our. reveler element when a new page loads. We could create a gap animation directly. on each page, but that would mean. repeating the same code multiple times. Instead, let's create a custom hook that. encapsulates this animation logic. We'll. call it use reer. This approach will. make our code much cleaner and easier to. maintain. If you want to change the. animation later, we only need to update. it in one place. Let's start by adding. the use client directive at the top of. our file. This directive is necessary. because we are using browser APIs. through gap. It tells nextJS that this. code should only run on the client side,. not during the server side rendering. So. you would need to define this directive,. otherwise it will throw errors. Next,. I'll import the tools we need from GSAP. the use gap hook for react integration,. the main gap object and customize for. more control over our animation timing. Now I need to register the custom is. plug-in with gap so we can use it. I'm. also creating a custom using cow called. hop with a cubic pzier cow. This creates. that smooth elegant motion that gives. our transition that polished feel. Now. for our actual oak function use revealer. inside I'm using the use gap hook which. automatically sets up and cleans up gap. animations. The animation itself is. simple. We are targeting the revealer. glass and animating its scale Y property. from one down to zero. This creates the. effect of the dark overlay retracting. upward. I have set the duration to 1.25. seconds for a smooth feel and added a. delay of 1 second to accommodate the. clip path and scale animations we'll. cover next. Finally, I'm using our. custom hop easing for the distinctive. motion. Now we just need to go to each. of our page components, import this. hook, and call. it. When each page loads, the revealer. will automatically animate out toward. the top, revealing our page content,. initiating the transition.

effect. Now let's create the key part of. the transition. The part where the new. page content reveals itself. First we. need to install a library called next. review transitions. This library makes. it easier to implement view transitions. API with next.js app router. Once it's. installed let's update our nav. component. We'll start by adding the use. client directive at the top since we are. using browser APIs. Next I'll import two. key hooks. Use transition router from. next view transitions and use path name. from next.js. The use transition router. hook will give us control over page. transitions and use path name will tell. us which page we are currently on. This. is important because we don't want to. trigger transitions when clicking on the. current pages link. Inside our nav. component, I'll initialize this hooks. I'm creating a router object with use. transition router and getting the. current path name with use path name. hook. Now let's create a function called. trigger page transition. This is where. the magic happens. I'm using the web. animations API to animate the documents. root element. The animation uses clip. path to create that expanding rectangle. effect. We start with a thin horizontal. line below the middle of the screen. That's what this clip path creates. Then. we animate to a full screen rectangle. The animation runs for 2 seconds. I'm. using a custom cubic basier curve. Same. as the custom is property we created. The key part is the pseudo element. property set to view transitions new. route. This tells the browser to apply. this animation to the new page as it. transitions in. Next, I'll create a. handle navigation function that manages. our link clicks. It takes a path. parameter and returns an event handler. function. First, it checks if we are. clicking a link to the current page. If. so, it prevents the default action and. returns early no need to transition to. the same page. Otherwise, it uses a. transition router to navigate to the new. path. The important part is the on. transition ready call back. This. triggers our page transition animation. when the new page is ready to show. Finally, let's update our JSX to use.

this navigation handler. I'll add an. onclick event listener to each link. component in our navigation. When the. route changes, you can see the current. page still disappears immediately, which. isn't what we want. To fix this, we need. to make some updates to our CSS to. control the default behavior of the view. transitions. Let's add the CSS rules. Now, first, I'm targeting both the old. and new root elements. I'm setting their. animation property to none and override. the default animations. item.title: The GTA 6 Landing Page Scroll Animation That Broke The Internet
item.itunes:title: The GTA 6 Landing Page Scroll Animation That Broke The Internet
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講者收到了許多觀眾的要求，希望示範如何重現GTA 6網站上令人驚豔的捲動動畫。他表示，這個效果非常吸引人，也因此會是個有趣的挑戰。這個動畫的特色是，當使用者向下捲動時，Logo和內容會平滑地消失，同時英雄圖片會向外縮放。接著，Logo會以類似挖空視窗的方式逐漸顯現，並透過其形狀揭示背景。再向下捲動，文字會以平滑的漸變效果出現。講者承認，重建這個效果比預期的複雜，經過七個小時的努力和四種不同的嘗試後，他成功地利用SVG、GSAP和Scroll Trigger重現了這個動畫效果。
他採用了SVG遮罩的方法來實現揭示效果。他建立了一個SVG遮罩，其中包含定義Logo形狀的路徑，然後將此遮罩應用到覆蓋視窗的矩形上，創造了一個Logo形狀的「窗口」，這個窗口會顯示下方的內容，並隱藏其他所有內容。影片將逐步示範如何重現相同的效果。
接著，講者提到了提供源碼的贊助方案，並鼓勵觀眾點讚和訂閱。

素材準備


首先，講者說明了專案所需的素材：英雄區塊的背景圖片、前景圖片（人物角色分離在透明背景上）、Logo的PNG版本、以及Logo的SVG版本。SVG版本尤其重要，需要從設計工具匯出，並提取路徑資料。為了方便操作，他建立了一個名為logo.js的獨立檔案，將這個路徑資料匯出為名為logoData的字串變數。這個logoData將被用於建立遮罩，透過Logo形狀來顯示背景。

HTML結構


接下來，講者概述了HTML結構。主要分為兩個部分：英雄區塊，包含動畫；以及在捲動後出現的後續區塊。
英雄區塊中，首先加入一個具有hero image container class的div，用於容納分層的圖片。在這個容器中，背景圖片作為第一個元素。接下來，加入一個具有hero image logo class的div，其中包含Logo圖片。這個Logo初始可見，但會在捲動時淡出。第三個元素是前景圖片，也就是分離的人物角色圖片。在這些圖片下方，增加一個具有hero image copy class的div，包含一段文字。
在圖片容器之外，增加一個fade overlay div，用於創建淡入效果。關鍵的元素是具有overlay class的div，其中包含SVG。在這個SVG中，定義了一個名為logo re mask的遮罩，包含一個全螢幕的矩形和一個具有ID為logo mask的路徑元素。這個路徑將使用JavaScript中的logoData填充。然後，將這個遮罩應用於填充深色的全螢幕矩形上，創造遮罩效果。
此外，還有一個空的logo container div，用於定位Logo遮罩。接著是包含標題文字的overlay copy div，文字會在捲動序列中稍後出現。最後，外部區塊將包含一些佔位符文字，在捲動完動畫後可見。

CSS樣式設定


接下來，講者開始樣式設定。首先，他導入了Google Fonts的DMsense字體。然後，重置所有預設的瀏覽器邊距和填充，並確保使用border box的盒模型。
接著，設定body的字體、深色背景和白色文字。他隱藏了水平溢出，以避免不必要的滾動條。他將所有圖片設定為佔據容器的100%寬度和高度，並使用object fit cover來保持長寬比。
對於排版，他將H1元素設定為大寫，使用大字體和緊密的行距，以及稍微負值的字母間距，營造現代感。段落也被設定為大寫，但字體較小且使用中等粗細的字重。
每個區塊都會佔據整個視窗的寬度和高度，並使用相對定位，以便對其中的元素進行絕對定位。背景顏色與body顏色相同，並且隱藏英雄圖片容器及其樣式圖片的溢出，以及fade overlay。他將它們進行絕對定位，以填充整個容器。
Logo圖片進行特殊處理，從頂部定位20%，並水平居中。他設定了特定的寬度，並允許高度自動調整，同時保持長寬比。捲動提示文字位於螢幕底部並居中。
fade overlay具有白色背景，會在捲動時逐漸改變不透明度。包含SVG遮罩的overlay被絕對定位，高度為200%，並使用Z軸索引1，確保其位於圖片之上。設定了transform origin為水平中心和垂直15%，用於控制縮放。
對於logo container，定位為從頂部算起25%，並水平居中，這決定了Logo遮罩出現的位置。他提到，這個值需要根據不同的Logo進行調整，並解釋了他的Logo中眼睛的設計。
包含文字的overlay copy位於視窗底部附近，並使用Z軸索引2，確保其出現在其他元素之上。對於此文字，使用了background clip技術來建立文字遮罩，以便稍後在JavaScript中顯示漸變背景。Transform origin設定為中心上方，控制動畫期間的縮放。
[音樂]
後續區塊的文字僅使用flex佈局水平和垂直居中。最後，針對較小的螢幕添加了一些響應式調整，縮小字體，並確保overlay copy在行動裝置上佔據全部寬度。

JavaScript程式碼


講者開始講解JavaScript，使頁面動起來。首先，他導入了必要的依賴項，包括logo.js中的SVG路徑資料、用於動畫的GSAP、用於觸發基於滾動位置動畫的GSAP的Scroll Trigger插件、以及用於平滑滾動體驗的Lenis。
導入後，他將Scroll Trigger插件註冊到GSAP，以便使用。然後，他將所有內容包裝在DOM content loaded事件監聽器中，確保程式碼在頁面完全載入後運行。
在事件監聽器中，他設定了Lenis平滑滾動，並讓Lenis在發生滾動時更新Scroll Trigger。他使用了現成的程式碼。
接著，他選取了所有DOM元素，包括背景圖片容器、起始Logo、捲動提示文字、白色overlay、包含遮罩的SVG overlay、以及標題文字。這些選取用於操作這些元素，使其隨著使用者捲動而改變。
接下來，他設定Logo遮罩及其尺寸。他定義了overlay的初始縮放值為350，這個數值決定了遮罩開始時的縮放程度，這個數值可以根據Logo類型進行調整。他選取了在HTML中定義的logo container，以及SVG中的logo mask元素。
接著是一個重要的步驟，他將SVG路徑的D屬性設定為使用匯入的logoData，這定義了SVG遮罩中Logo的實際形狀。
為了正確定位Logo，需要確定容器和SVG路徑的大小和位置。他使用get bounding client rect函式獲取容器的確切位置和尺寸，以及get bounding box方法獲取Logo形狀的尺寸。然後，他計算縮放因子。因為SVG Logo路徑需要正確地適合容器。他將容器的寬度除以路徑的寬度，得到水平縮放比例，並將容器的高度除以路徑的高度，得到垂直縮放比例。
================================================

In the last few days, a bunch of folks. reached out requesting a video on this. stunning scroll animation from the GTA 6. website. When I first saw it, even I was. instantly captivated and knew it would. be an exciting challenge to recreate. this effect from scratch. As you can. see, when scrolling down, the logo and. the content of this section smoothly. fade away while the hero image zooms. out. Then, the logo cinematically starts. coming into view as a cutout window. while still revealing the background. through its shape. As you scroll more,. some text emerges with a smooth gradient. fade. I'm not going to lie, when I. started trying to rebuild this, it. turned out way more complex than I. expected. But after about 7 hours and. trying out like four different. approaches, I was finally able to. replicate the scroll animation using. SVGs, GSAP, and scroll trigger. The. technique I landed on uses an SVG. masking approach for the reveal effect. Basically, I created an SVG mask with a. path inside it that defines the logo. shape and then applied this mask to a. rectangle covering the view port that. created sort of a window in the shape of. the logo that reveals what's underneath. while hiding everything else. In this. video, I'll show you step by step how to. recreate the same effect yourself. If. you find these videos helpful, a like. and subscribe would really mean a lot. Also, if you want to access the source. code for this project, plus hundreds of. other similar micro projects along with. the complete website template every. month, you can check out the pro. membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's dive in. Before we dive into coding, let's go. through the assets we'll need for this. project. First, we need a background. image for hero section. I'm using this. vibrant GTA themed artwork from 4K. wallpapers.com, but you can substitute. any image you prefer. Next, we need a. foreground image with just the. characters isolated on a transparent. background. This is crucial for our. layering effect. It allows us to place. the logo behind the characters, but in. front of the background. We'll stack. these elements with the background at. the bottom, logo in the middle, and.

character image on the top. Next, for. the logo, I have created the simple PNG. version in Figma. You can use any logo. design that works for your project. Most. importantly, you'll also need an SVG. version of your logo. After exporting. from your design tool, open the SVG in. any browser and extract the path data. from it. This path information is. essential for creating our mask effect. To make this easier, I've created a. separate file called logo.js that. exports this path data as a string. variable named logo data. We'll import. this into our main script and use it to. create the mask that reles our. background through the logo shape. With. these assets prepared, we are ready to. start building our HTML structure. We'll need two main sections. A hero. section which will contain our animation. and an outro section that appears after. scrolling. Inside the hero section, I'm. first adding a div with the class hero. image container that would hold our. layered images. Within this container,. I'm placing the background image as the. first element. This will be our main. backdrop. Next, I'm adding another div. with the class name hero image logo. which contains our logo image. This logo. will initially be visible but will fade. out as we scroll. Then I'm adding our. second layer image. This is isolated. character image that will appear on top. of everything else. Below that I'm. adding a div with the class hero image. copy containing a paragraph text. Now. outside the image container I'm adding a. fade overlay div which will help create. a fade effect as we scroll. The next. element is crucial a div with the class. name overlay containing an SVG. This is. where the magic happens. Inside the SVG,. I have defined a mask called logo re. mask which contains a full screen. rectangle and a path element with the ID. logo mask. This path will be populated. with our logo data from JavaScript. Then. I'm applying this mask to a full screen. rectangle filled with a dark color. This. creates our mask effect where the logo. shape will reveal the content. underneath. I'm also adding an empty. logo container div which will be used as. a reference point for positioning our. logo mask. Then there is an overlay copy. div containing an H1 with a text that.

will appear later in the scroll. sequence. Finally, the outer section. will have some placeholder text that. will be visible after scrolling past the. animation. That would be it for the HTML. structure. Let's get to styling. now. First, you can see I've imported. the DMsense font from Google Fonts. Now. let's start by resetting all default. browser margins, paddings, and ensuring. box sizing is set to border box. For the. body, I'm setting our font family to. DMs, giving it a dark background color,. and white text. I'm also hiding any. horizontal overflow to prevent unwanted. scroll bars. I'm setting all images to. take up 100% of their containers width. and height with object fit cover to. maintain aspect ratios. For typography,. I'm styling the H1 elements to be. uppercase, giving a large font size with. tight line spacing and slightly negative. letter spacing for that modern. look. Paragraphs are also styled to be. uppercase but smaller and with medium. font. weight. Each section will take up the. full view port width and height with. relative positioning. So, we can. absolutely position elements inside. them. I'm setting the background to. match our body color and hiding any. overflow for our hero image container. and its styled images as well as the. fade overlay. I'm absolutely positioning. them to fill the entire. container. The logo image gets special. treatment. It's positioned 20% from the. top and centered horizontally. I am setting a specific width and. allowing the height to adjust. automatically while maintaining the. aspect. ratio. The scroll prompt text is. positioned at the bottom of the screen. and. centered. I'm adding will change opacity. to optimize the animation. performance. Our fade overlay is given a white. background and will gradually change. opacity during. scrolling. The overlay that contains our. SVG mask is positioned absolutely but. with 200% height and a Z index of one to. ensure it sits above our images. I am. setting its transform origin to the. center horizontally and 15% vertically.

which will control how it scales. Now. for the logo container, this is crucial. I'm positioning it fixed at 25% from the. top and centered horizontally. This. position is important because it. determines where your logo mask will. appear. You'll likely need to adjust. this value based on your specific logo. In my case, I wanted the zoom effect to. happen through the eye character present. in the last line in my logo. So, 25%. worked well. Different logos will. require different positioning. So, be. prepared to experiment with this value. The overlay copy that contains our text. is positioned near the bottom of the. viewport with a zindex of two to ensure. it appears above the other. elements. For this text, I'm using the. background clip technique to create a. text mask that will reveal a gradient. background later in our. JavaScript. I'm setting the transform. origin to the center top which will. control how it scales during animation. [Music]. The outro section text is simply. centered horizontally and vertically. using flex. layout. Finally, I'm adding some. responsive adjustments for smaller. screens, reducing font sizes, and. ensuring the overlay copy takes the full. width on mobile. devices. Let's get to JavaScript now and. bring this page to life. First, I'm importing the necessary. dependencies. The logo data from our. logojs file which contains the SVG path. data. Gap for handling our animations. Scroll trigger plug-in for GSAP which. will trigger animations based on scroll. position. Lenis, a smooth scrolling. library to make the experience more. polished. After importing these, I'm. registering the scroll trigger plug-in. with GSA so we can use it. Now I'm. wrapping everything in a DOM content. loaded event listener to make sure our. code only runs after the page has fully. loaded. Inside this event listener I'm. setting up Lennis source mode scrolling. will create a new Lennis instance and. tells it to update scroll trigger. whenever scrolling happens. You can find. this block of code on the documentation. website. I've used it as is without any. change.

Now I'm selecting all DOM elements. We'll need to animate the container with. our background images, the logo that. appears at the beginning, the scroll. down text, the white overlay that will. fade in, the SVG overlay that contains. our mask, the heading text that will. appear later in the animation. These. selections give us references to. manipulate these elements as the user. scrolls. Next, I'm setting up the logo. mask and its dimensions. I start by. defining an initial scale value of 350. for our overlay. This is a really large. number that determines how zoomed in the. mask will be at the beginning. You can. play around this value based on your. logo type. I am selecting the logo. container we defined in our HTML and the. logo mask element from our SVG. Now. comes an important step. I'm setting the. D attribute of our SVG path to use the. logo data we imported. This is what. defines the actual shape of our logo in. the SVG mask. To position the logo. properly, I need to figure out both the. size and the position of our container. and the SVG path. For the container, I'm. using get bounding client rect function. which gives me its exact position and. dimensions on the page. For the SVG. path, I'm using get bounding box method. which tells me the dimensions of our. logo shape. Now I'm calculating scaling. factors. This is important because our. SVG logo path needs to properly fit. within our container. I'm dividing the container's width by. the path's width to get the horizontal. scale and the container's height by the. path height to get the vertical. scale. Then I'm taking the smaller one. to preserve the logo shape without. stretching it. The positioning. calculations help center our logo. I'm. starting with the container position,. then adding an offset to center the. logo, and finally adjusting for the. logo's internal coordinates. This ensures the logo appears exactly. where we want it in our. mask. Finally, I'm applying these. calculations to position and scale our. SVG path using the transform attribute. This tells the browser exactly where to. place our logo mask and how large it. should be. These precise calculations. are what allow our effect to work with.

any logo shape and size. Without them,. the mask wouldn't align properly and our. reveal effect wouldn't work correctly. Next, I'm creating our scroll trigger. instance. This is what will control all. of our effects as the user scrolls down. the page. I'll first set up the scroll. trigger with some important options. I'm. using hero section as the trigger. element, which means our animation will. start when this section enters the. viewport. I'm setting start to top. So,. the animation begins right away when the. top of our hero section reaches the top. of the viewport. For the end value, I'm. setting it to five times the viewport. height. This means our animation will. take place over a long scroll distance,. giving us plenty of room to create a. smooth gradual effect. I'm also enabling. pin, which keeps our hero section fixed. on screen while the animation plays, and. pin spacing, which maintains the scroll. height. The scrub value of one creates a. slight delay between scrolling and the. animation updating, making it feel. smoother. Next, I'm defining what. happens during the scroll in the. onupdate call back. This function. receives a self parameter that contains. information about the scroll progress. I'll start by getting the current scroll. progress which is a value between 0 and. 1 representing how far through the. animation we are. Then I'm calculating. the fade opacity for our initial. elements for the first 15% of scrolling. I want the logo and scroll text to. gradually fade out. I'm calculating this. with a formula that converts our. progress to an opacity value between 1. and zero. If this scroll progress is. below 15%, I'm setting the opacity of. these elements based on our calculation. Otherwise, I'm setting the opacity to. zero to keep them hidden. Next, for the. first 85% of scrolling, I'm handling the. scaling effects. First, I'm normalizing. the progress to a 01 range within this. portion of the scroll. I'm calculating. the scale of our hero image container,. starting at 1.5 and gradually reducing. to one as we scroll. This creates that. zoom out effect for the background. For. the overlay scale, I'm using a formula. that starts at our large initial overlay. scale value and gradually reduces it to. one. The power function helps create a.

smooth exponential transition instead of. a linear one. This is what creates the. effect of zooming out through the logo. shape. After 25% of scrolling, I start fading. in our white overlay. I'm calculating. its opacity to smoothly transition from. 0 to 1 between 25% and 65% of the. scroll. Finally, I'm applying these calculated. values using GAP's set method, which. updates the properties right away. Now,. for the final part of our scroll. animation, I'm adding the text reveal. effect that happens between 60% and 85%. of our scroll progress. When the scroll. progress is between 6 and 85, I want to. gradually reveal our heading text with a. smooth gradient effect. First, I'm. calculating a normalized progress value. just for this specific section of the. scroll. Converting it from a 0 to one. range. I'm setting a gradient spread of. 100, which determines how sharp or. gradual our text will be. Then, I'm. calculating the position of the. gradient's bottom edge, starting at 240. and moving upward as we scroll. The. gradient's top edge is positioned 100. pixels above the bottom edge. Then, I'm. scaling the text slightly as it appears,. starting from 1.25 25 times its normal. size and reducing it to one as you. scroll. This creates a subtle zoom. effect on the text itself. Now comes the. cool part. I am setting a linear. gradient as the background for our text. The gradient starts with a dark. background color at the top, then. transitions to a reddish color at the. bottom. The gradient positions are. dynamically calculated based on our. scroll progress. Because we set the text. to use background clip text in our CSS,. only the text itself shows this. gradient. As we scroll, the gradient. moves upward through the text, creating. that smooth rival effect from bottom to. top. I'm also gradually increasing the. opacity of the entire text element as we. scroll through this section. If the. scroll progress is less than 6, I'm. keeping the text completely invisible by. setting its opacity to zero. And with. that, our scroll trigger setup is. complete. This configuration creates the. entire animated sequence. Hope you found. the video helpful. See you in the next.

one.

====

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lh5fQIlyOe0

Learn how to recreate GTA VI's mind-blowing scroll animation using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP and ScrollTrigger! This step-by-step tutorial shows you how to build that cinematic logo reveal effect that everyone's talking about.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Rockstar Games

Website: https://www.rockstargames.com/VI

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
